coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a bifunctional fluorescent chiral composite based on magnetic Fe3O4/chiral carbon dots@hierarchical porous metal-organic framework
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Min
Zhou, Meiling
Wang, Min
Du, Jiayin
Liu, Chunlan
Wang, Yue
Xia, Zhining
coredata.dc:description: Considering the selective pharmacological activity and ecotoxicity of chiral drugs, the development of chiral materials with the dual functions of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption is of great significance. Herein, a novel bifunctional chiral composite (Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8) which does not contain expensive and rare fluorescent chiral ligands or metal ions, was constructed for the first time by encapsulating chiral carbon dots (CCDs) and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles into hierarchical porous metal-organic frameworks (HP-MOFs). Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, which integrates fluorescent chiral property, magnetism, and hierarchical porosity, shows enormous potential in enantiomeric recognition and adsorption. Fluorescence detection results demonstrate that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 presents different fluorescence quenching for naproxen enantiomers. The limits of detection are determined to be 0.05 μM for S-naproxen (S-Nap) and 0.30 μM for R-naproxen (R-Nap), respectively. Furthermore, the isothermal, kinetic, and thermodynamic adsorption behaviors of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers were systematically studied. Due to its hierarchical porosity, the composite exhibits higher adsorption capacity to naproxen enantiomers compared to the non-hierarchical porous composite. Studies of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption mechanisms affirm that the synergistic effect of multiple mechanisms exists between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen enantiomers. Finally, the satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviations in the actual sample assays demonstrate the practicality of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for S-Nap detection. This non-destructive functionalization method creates an innovative pathway for developing advanced multifunctional chiral materials, holding great promise for enantiomeric recognition and adsorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral composite
Chiral carbon dots
Hierarchical porous metal-organic frameworks
Encapsulation
Enantiomeric recognition
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and chirality of CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8
Exploration of the enantiomer recognition mechanism
Enantioselective fluorescence response of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to other chiral compounds
Determination of S-Nap in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974015 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CCDs (A, B). The inset pictures show the particle size distribution and enlarged TEM image of CCDs, respectively. C.TEM images of Fe3O4 NPs. The inset picture is the enlarged TEM image of Fe3O4 NPs. TEM images of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (D, E).

A. XRD patterns of Fe3O4 NPs, CCDs, HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. B. FT-IR spectra of CCDs, HP-ZIF-8, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. C. TGA curves of HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. D. VSM hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (the inset picture is the separation of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 under the magnetic field).
A. UV–vis absorption spectra of the CCDs, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs under different excitations. C. Excitation and emission bands of CCDs.

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 under different excitations. B. Excitation and emission bands of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. C. Fluorescence spectra of CCDs, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, and the mixture of HP-ZIF-8 + CCDs + Fe3O4 NPs. D. CD spectra of L-Cys, CCDs, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8.

A. Fluorescence spectra change of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 with naproxen enantiomers. Fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 in the presence of different concentrations (0–30 μM) of S-Nap (B) and R-Nap (C); D. Linear correlation between F0/F of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen enantiomers concentrations.

A. Fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 in the presence of different enantiomeric percentages of S-Nap. B. Linear correlation between F0/F of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and the enantiomeric percentage of S-Nap. C. Chiral recognition ability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers in the interfering matrix. D. Reusability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8.

A. Adsorption isotherms of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers. B. Adsorption kinetics of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers. Linear plots of ln (Qe/Ce) vs Qe for S-Nap (C) and R-Nap (D) at different temperatures.

A. XRD patterns of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap. B. UV–vis spectra of S-Nap and fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. C. Fluorescence lifetime of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-R-Nap. D. CD spectra of S-Nap, R-Nap, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-R-Nap.
High resolutions XPS spectra of (A) C1s (B) N1s (C) O1s and (D) Zn2p for Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 before and after the adsorption of S-Nap.

A. PZC of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. B. Adsorption capacities of Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8, Fe3O4/CCDs@ZIF-8, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers. C. Fluorescence response of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to different chiral substances.
Schematic diagram of synthesis and application of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8.
Determination of S-Nap in actual samples (n = 5).

row.entry: Initial S-Nap amount determined by HPLC (μM)
Initial S-Nap amount determined by Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (μM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: -: not detected. RSD: relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a bifunctional fluorescent chiral composite based on magnetic Fe3O4/chiral carbon dots@hierarchical porous metal-organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Natural Product Synthesis and Drug Research, Innovative Drug Research Center, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Natural Product Synthesis and Drug Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Natural Product Synthesis and Drug Research, Innovative Drug Research Center, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the selective pharmacological activity and ecotoxicity of chiral drugs, the development of chiral materials with the dual functions of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption is of great significance. Herein, a novel bifunctional chiral composite (Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8) which does not contain expensive and rare fluorescent chiral ligands or metal ions, was constructed for the first time by encapsulating chiral carbon dots (CCDs) and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles into hierarchical porous metal-organic frameworks (HP-MOFs). Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, which integrates fluorescent chiral property, magnetism, and hierarchical porosity, shows enormous potential in enantiomeric recognition and adsorption. Fluorescence detection results demonstrate that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 presents different fluorescence quenching for naproxen enantiomers. The limits of detection are determined to be 0.05 μM for S-naproxen (S-Nap) and 0.30 μM for R-naproxen (R-Nap), respectively. Furthermore, the isothermal, kinetic, and thermodynamic adsorption behaviors of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers were systematically studied. Due to its hierarchical porosity, the composite exhibits higher adsorption capacity to naproxen enantiomers compared to the non-hierarchical porous composite. Studies of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption mechanisms affirm that the synergistic effect of multiple mechanisms exists between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen enantiomers. Finally, the satisfactory recoveries and relative standard deviations in the actual sample assays demonstrate the practicality of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for S-Nap detection. This non-destructive functionalization method creates an innovative pathway for developing advanced multifunctional chiral materials, holding great promise for enantiomeric recognition and adsorption.

A novel bifunctional chiral composite (Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8) was constructed for the first time by encapsulating chiral carbon dots (CCDs) and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles into hierarchical porous metal-organic frameworks (HP-MOFs). Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, integrating the fluorescent chiral property, magnetism, and hierarchical porosity was applied for enantiomeric recognition and adsorption of naproxen enantiomers.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Enantiomers of chiral drugs have markedly different pharmacological activities and pharmacokinetic properties. Rigorous identification of the enantiomers of chiral drugs is essential for clinical monitoring and drug production [1,2]. Moreover, the enantioselective ecotoxicity of chiral drugs cannot be ignored as chiral drugs are pseudo-persistent in the environment and potentially lead to human health issues through water and foodborne contamination [3]. Thus, it is crucial to develop an efficient strategy for enantiomeric recognition and adsorption of chiral drugs. Notably, the fabrication of fluorescent chiral material which possesses the dual functions of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption holds great promise.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) which have exhibited good performance in sensing and adsorption due to outstanding properties such as facile synthesis, ultra-high porosity, large surface area, framework structure diversity, and functional tunability, have gained various attention [4–6]. Especially, fluorescent chiral MOFs, as a subclass of MOFs, combine the inherent features of MOFs and the special characteristics of chirality and fluorescence property, resulting in growing attention on the potential of enantiomeric recognition and adsorption [7,8]. For example, Li et al. have introduced chiral center l-histidine and fluorescent center Eu3+ into the Zr-based MOFs through post-synthetic ligand exchange to obtain chiral fluorescent MOFs, and the fluorescent sensor can be employed to effectively detect tryptophan enantiomers [9]. Zhang et al. have designed and constructed a 2D homochiral MOF (HMOF-3) by using homochiral organic ligand and Zn salts, and the 2D nanosheets (HMOF-NS) obtained by solvent-assisted sonication exfoliation of HMOF-3 exhibit high sensitivity, enantioselectivity, and good recoverability for enantiomeric substances [10]. Nevertheless, the study on fluorescent chiral MOFs is still in its infancy, and there is a lack of research on novel synthesis strategies and the potential for fluorescence recognition and adsorption of enantiomers.

Currently, preparation difficulties, mainly reflected in the introduction of chiral and fluorescent sources, have gradually become a major challenge in the development of fluorescent chiral MOFs. In general, the chirality of fluorescent chiral MOFs can be introduced through three strategies including direct synthesis, post-synthesis strategy, and chiral induction. However, these strategies still face the following limitations: (1) In the direct synthesis method, fluorescent chiral MOFs are obtained from the enantiopure ligands. Shortcomings such as the expensive enantiopure ligands, reduced effective nanopore space of MOFs, compromised chiral integrity during the reaction, low surface area and limited chiral functions of obtained fluorescent chiral MOFs greatly hinder the promotion of this method [11–13]. (2) In the post-synthesis approach, the functional diversity of fluorescent chiral MOFs can be broadened, but the key to this approach is that the parent MOFs must possess functional groups or open metal sites that can easily react with chiral reagents. (3) The chiral induction strategy also allows for preparation of fluorescent chiral MOFs from achiral MOFs while maintaining almost unchanged backbone structure of the MOFs [14]. However, the non-universal applicability of chiral templates and the possible reactions between functional groups of organic ligands and chiral reagents make the use of this method challenging. On the other hand, introduction of fluorescent sources of fluorescent chiral MOFs can be achieved by using fluorescent organic linkers, fluorescent metal ions, and exogenous fluorescent molecules [15]. Among them, fluorescent chiral organic linkers and fluorescent chiral metal ions are very scarce and difficult to obtain. Furthermore, the resulting fluorescent chiral MOFs may have the possibility of racemization, leading in poor chiral optical purity [16]. The inadequacy in current preparation methods for fluorescent chiral MOFs presents significant obstacles to their development, making it urgent to explore novel synthesis strategies.

A feasible strategy can be proposed that by utilizing achiral MOFs as hosts to encapsulate chiral fluorescent guests based on MOF-exogenous fluorescent molecule systems. Notably, in this non-destructive functionalization strategy, the choice of fluorescent chiral guest is crucial, as it offers the possibility to endow composite charming properties. Chiral carbon dots (CCDs), possessing both chirality and fluorescent property, have captured our attention. In recent years, due to the excellent stability, chiral optical property, and diversity of chiral precursors, CCDs offer us an exciting potential to recognize enantiomers and have been progressively developed for fluorescence identification of enantiomers [17–20]. Nowadays, research attention has gradually turned to the CCDs-based composite that can endow CCDs with better performance and greatly expand the application of CCDs. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, researches on the preparation and responsive behaviors of CCDs-based composite are still at an early stage and the CCDs-based composite possessing multifunctionality (e.g., enantioselective recognition, adsorption, and storage) has not been reported [21]. It can be speculated that constructing MOFs-encapsulated CCDs composite is a reasonable approach to overcome the inadequacy of current fluorescent chiral MOFs and to expand the application of CCDs. CCDs have outstanding advantages in the preparation of MOFs-based chiral composite, which are mainly manifested in three aspects: (1) The cost of the composite prepared using CCDs is significantly cheaper than that of consisting of expensive and synthetically complex chiral and fluorescent centers. (2) The wide variety of available chiral precursors enriches the diversity of MOFs-encapsulated CCDs composites and expands their application. (3) CCDs that combine chirality and fluorescence property can avoid the potential signal response effects when both chiral and fluorescence centers are present independently in the composite, thereby facilitating the fluorescence recognition of the enantiomers. Moreover, encapsulating CCDs within MOFs may overcome environmental interference, interfering substances, and aggregation of CCDs on the fluorescent chiral property of CCDs, ultimately enhancing the stability. Therefore, this proposed synthesis strategy can kill two birds with one stone.

In addition, another thing to note is that microporous MOFs exhibit limited pore size, which may lead to constrained encapsulation efficiency of CCDs and limited rapid diffusion of analytes. Hierarchical porous MOFs (HP-MOFs) with multiple types of pore structures can increase the specific surface area and interaction sites of MOFs, thereby improving encapsulation efficiency and reducing mass transfer resistance for small molecule analytes [22–24]. It is reasonable to speculate that the introduction of HP-MOFs into CCDs-based composite enables the adsorption and sensing performance of analytes to be improved compared to microporous MOFs. Furthermore, the introduction of magnetism ensures the rapid separation and recovery of the composite from solution by an external magnetic field. Accordingly, these materials have exactly advantages in enhancing chiral composite performance for enantiomeric recognition and adsorption, which motivates us to propose the coupled construction of CCDs, HP-MOFs, and magnetic nanoparticles.

Herein, for the first time, a novel strategy to achieve enantiomeric recognition and adsorption by encapsulating CCDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) into HP-MOFs was developed. The applicability of this method was explored by using naproxen enantiomers with enantioselective pharmacological activity and ecotoxicity as a model chiral drug. The illustration of the fabrication and application of the chiral composite (Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8) is shown in Scheme 1 . Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 displays enhanced photostability and satisfactory enantioselective recognition and adsorption towards naproxen enantiomers. The response mechanism between naproxen enantiomers and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was systematically studied, and the practicability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for the determination of S-naproxen (S-Nap) in actual samples was analyzed. This work provides a promising strategy for fabricating a new generation of functional chiral materials.
The information of materials and instruments are shown in Electronic Supplementary Information.

The synthesis of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs are described in Electronic Supplementary Information. Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was synthesized through a facile one-step synthesis method. In detail, 2 mL of NaOH was added to 1.6 mL of Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O (0.84 M, 1.3 mM). After a white precipitate was observed, 5 mg of Fe3O4 NPs, 16 mL of 2-methylimidazole solution (3 M, 48 mM), 2 mL of CCDs, and 28.4 mL of deionized water were added. And then, the mixed solution reacted at room temperature with stirring for 2 h. After the reaction, the products were washed several times with deionized water. Finally, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was obtained and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

For comparison, Fe3O4/CCDs@ZIF-8, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8, and HP-ZIF-8 were prepared through the same method without adding NaOH, CCDs, and both Fe3O4 NPs and CCDs, respectively. The mixture of HP-ZIF-8 + CCDs + Fe3O4 NPs was prepared according to the following method: 50 mL of a mixed solution containing 100 mg of prepared HP-ZIF-8, 5 mg of Fe3O4 NPs and 2 mL of CCDs solution was reacted under stirring for 2 h. The products were washed several times with deionized water and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

A series of fluorescence measurements were performed as follows: 5 mg of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water to form the stock solution by ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, 2.7 mL of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 stock solution and 0.3 mL of different concentrations of R-Nap/S-Nap solution were introduced into the 1 × 1 cm2 spectrofluorimetric cell for fluorescence experiments. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength at 340 nm. The F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of chiral enantiomers, respectively, and the F0/F value is used as the analytical signal for R-Nap/S-Nap recognition by Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the enantiomers (FR/FS) is used to express the enantioselectivity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. The limit of detection (LOD) was analyzed by the equation of LOD = 3σ/K, where K is the slope of the calibration curve, and σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. Moreover, the common ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, NO3 −, SO4 2− and sugars (fructose, glucose, and sucrose) were utilized to study the anti-interference ability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8.

The adsorption behavior was evaluated through isothermal, kinetic, and thermodynamic experiments. In detail, the batch adsorption experiments were performed by adding 2 mg of absorbent to 8 mL of different concentrations of naproxen enantiomers solutions. The mixture was shaken for 180 min, and then was separated by an external magnet. After filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane, the concentration of residual naproxen enantiomers in the solution was determined by HPLC. The initial concentrations of naproxen were in the range of 2.5–70 mg/L in the adsorption isotherm experiments. In the adsorption kinetic experiments, the initial concentration of naproxen enantiomers was 60 mg/L and the samples were taken at scheduled time intervals (0–240 min). The adsorption thermodynamics studies were performed at 298, 308, and 318 K, and the other experimental conditions were the same as those of the adsorption isotherm experiments. The chromatographic conditions and calculations of the adsorption experiments are described in Electronic Supplementary Information.

The morphologies of CCDs, Fe3O4 NPs, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 were first characterized. As depicted in Fig. 1 A and B, CCDs are particles with an average diameter of 4.86 nm and have a typical graphene-like inter-lattice spacing of 0.32 nm. SEM (Figs. S1A and 1B) and TEM images (Fig. 1C, D, and 1E) indicate that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has rhombic dodecahedral structure and is loaded with CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs. Moreover, Fe and S elements are observed in the EDS spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (Fig. S1C), which confirms the successfully doping of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs. The XRD and FT-IR analyses were performed to further verify the formation of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. The XRD patterns of Fe3O4 NPs, CCDs, HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 are shown in Fig. 2 A. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 30.2°, 35.3°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 57.1°, and 62.6° of Fe3O4 NPs, corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystallographic planes of Fe3O4 (JCPDS Card No.19-0629), suggests the successful synthesis of crystalline magnetic nanoparticles [25]. In the case of CCDs, the XRD pattern shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 28.3°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, revealing that CCDs contain a graphite-like structure [26]. The XRD pattern of HP-ZIF-8 in this work is in agreement with that of the reported HP-ZIF-8 structure [27]. Both HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 exhibit a diffraction peak at 7.3°, which belongs to the (011) peak, indicating that the nanocrystals of HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 are composed of 12 facets with rhombic dodecahedral shape [28]. Similar diffraction peaks are observed in Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 compared to the diffraction pattern of HP-ZIF-8, which confirms that they have the same crystal structure and that the encapsulation of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs does not affect the crystal integrity of HP-ZIF-8 [29]. In addition, it is noteworthy that no significant diffraction peaks of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs are found in the XRD pattern of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. There are several possible reasons for the above phenomenon: (1) In comparison to the strong diffraction peaks of HP-ZIF-8, the diffraction peaks of individual CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs are relatively weak, rendering them indiscernible within the strong crystal diffraction spectra in the presence of HP-ZIF-8 [30,31]. (2) The low concentration and small size of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs hosted by the HP-ZIF-8 matrix do not lead to collapse of the HP-ZIF-8 framework [32,33]. Additionally, the slight peak attenuation observed on certain peaks may be due to the weak interaction between CCDs and HP-ZIF-8 [34]. (3) CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs may be distributed in the channels of the HP-ZIF-8 skeleton rather than being physically adsorbed onto its surface, which has been reported in other studies as well. Furthermore, as confirmed by TEM, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 possesses a host-guest encapsulated structure, which has minimal effect on the retention of structure and crystallinity of HP-ZIF-8 after encapsulating CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs [35–37]. The FT-IR spectra of CCDs, HP-ZIF-8, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 are delineated in Fig. 2B. CCDs show absorption peaks at 3431 cm−1, 3139 cm−1, 2520 cm−1, 1714 cm−1, 1587 cm−1, 1402 cm−1, and 1216 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, N–H, S–H, CO, CC, C–N, and C–O, respectively [26]. The FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is similar to that of HP-ZIF-8. The absorption bands at 1573 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, 1423 cm−1, 1141 cm−1, and 993 cm−1 prove the presence of imidazole ring. The peaks at 690 cm−1 and 424 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of Zn–N, respectively [38]. In addition, a new characteristic peak at 568 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of Fe–O in Fe3O4 NPs and the typical broad adsorption peaks ascribed to the stretching vibration of the O–H and CO in CCDs are observed, confirming the encapsulation of Fe3O4 NPs and CCDs in the HP-ZIF-8 framework. Moreover, the broader absorption peak at 3170 cm−1 in Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 compared with that in pure HP-ZIF-8 suggests weak interactions between CCDs and HP-ZIF-8. The obvious shifts from 3431 cm−1–3324 cm−1 for O–H and 1714 cm−1 to 1694 cm−1 for CO in Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 confirm the formation of hydrogen bonds [34,39]. Due to the conjugate system in CCDs and π-π stacking effect on HP-ZIF-8, peaks at 1587 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, and 1423 cm−1 move to lower wave numbers [40]. These results confirm the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8.

Subsequently, the porosity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was investigated. It can be seen from the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S2A) that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 exhibit a combination of type I and type IV isotherms, which corresponds to the presence of microporous and mesoporous skeleton structure. The pore size distribution (Fig. S2B) further confirms that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has both micropores and mesopores. In addition, the large surface area of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (1084.2 m2/g) may be conducive to the enrichment of chiral enantiomers. Further, the thermal behaviors of HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 was also studied. As portrayed in Fig. 2C, the TGA curves of HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 show the same phased weight loss process, indicating the integrity of HP-ZIF-8 frame structure after encapsulation of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs [41]. Moreover, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has a larger amount of weight loss compared with the HP-ZIF-8 sample, which may be due to the partial loss of CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs. Fig. 2D shows the magnetic properties of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4 NPs. The magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4 NPs exhibit superparamagnetic behavior with the saturated magnetization of 7.53 emu/g for Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and 45.91 emu/g for Fe3O4 NPs, respectively. The smaller magnetic property of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is attributed to the encapsulated Fe3O4 NPs. Nevertheless, the inset picture of Fig. 2D reveals that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 can still be easily separated from the aqueous solution with an externally applied magnetic field, indicating that the magnetic property of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is sufficient for magnetic separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and chirality of CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CCDs, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. CCDs have obvious absorption peaks at 245 and 350 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [42]. In the whole wavelength range, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8 has a very weak absorption band. The presence of characteristic absorption peaks of Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8 and CCDs in the spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 indicates the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 composite. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 shown in Fig. 3B, C, Fig. 4 A, B, and Fig. S3 reveal that both CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 have a maximum emission at 420 nm, excited by the maximum excitation wavelength at 340 nm. The same optical properties indicate that the preparation of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 does not affect the optical properties of CCDs. Moreover, Fig. 4C shows that Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8 does not exhibit any significant fluorescence, confirming that the fluorescence property of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 originates from CCDs. Furthermore, the mixture of HP-ZIF-8, CCDs, and Fe3O4 NPs does not show visible fluorescence, while Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 retains a strong fluorescence, indicating that CCDs are successfully encapsulated into HP-ZIF-8 rather than being adsorbed into the MOF cavities. The results of the photostability of CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 show that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has better photostability compared to CCDs (Fig. S4A-Fig. S4C), which may be related to the encapsulation of CCDs by HP-MOFs. Further, the chiral characteristics of CCDs and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 were investigated by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4D, the CD signal of CCDs located at 213 and 234 nm inherits the signal of L-Cys, and the appearance of these two peaks is attributed to the band splitting of L-Cys caused by one-pot carbonization. The new chiral center of CCDs at 350 nm may be induced by the optical transitions of CCD core states [43]. In addition, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 shows the same chiral center of CCDs, which confirms that the chiral composite still retains the chirality of CCDs, providing the necessary condition for further chiral recognition.

The experimental conditions (pH value, ionic strength, and reaction time) of chiral recognition to naproxen enantiomers were optimized. When the reaction time reaches 5 min and pH is 6.0, the maximum fluorescence enantioselectivity of naproxen enantiomers achieves (Fig. S4D-Fig. S4F). Moreover, the high ion concentration does not affect the interaction between naproxen enantiomers and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 exhibits a more significant decrease upon addition of S-Nap compared to R-Nap (Fig. 5 A), suggesting that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 can be employed as an efficient fluorescent sensor for enantioselective recognition of naproxen enantiomers. Moreover, with increasing concentrations of naproxen enantiomers, the fluorescence intensities of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 decrease obviously in different degrees (Fig. 5B and C). As demonstrated in Fig. 5D, the quenching efficiencies display two good linear increases with increasing concentrations of naproxen enantiomers. Two linear ranges of 0.1–7 and 8–30 μM in the high and low concentration ranges for S-Nap are separately determined with the correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9945 and 0.9929. For R-Nap, the linear ranges are 0.75–7 and 8–30 μM with R2 of 0.9927 and 0.9938, respectively. The LODs for S-Nap is 0.05 μM and that of R-Nap is 0.30 μM. In comparison with other methods for the identification of naproxen enantiomers, this work has satisfactory sensitivity (Table S1). It is worth mentioning that the high sensitivity of this method may be closely related to the hierarchical porosity and high surface area of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, which allow the accumulation of naproxen enantiomers and thus greatly increases the interaction.

The enantiomeric excess determinations were evaluated by measuring the fluorescence response of S-Nap/R-Nap at different enantiomeric percentages. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, the fluorescence intensity decreases with increasing percentage of S-Nap in the mixture solution, and the quenching efficiency exhibits a good linear relationship of different mole fractions of S-Nap. These results demonstrate that this work offers a practical method that enables not only the quantitative detection of S-Nap/R-Nap, but also the determination of the percentage of S-Nap/R-Nap in the naproxen enantiomer mixture. Moreover, the influences of common interferents (including e.g. common ions as well as sugars) on the recognition of naproxen enantiomers by Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 were also investigated. The negligible changes observed in naproxen enantiomeric recognition results (Fig. 6C) indicate the effective interference avoidance. Fig. 6D shows an insignificant decrease in fluorescence enantioselectivity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 after five cycles, suggesting the reusability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for the recognition of naproxen enantiomers.

Fig. 7 A clearly illustrates that the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 increases with the concentration of naproxen enantiomers, reaching equilibrium at a concentration of 60 mg/L. The maximum adsorption amounts for S-Nap/R-Nap are separately determined as 96.43 mg/g and 80.07 mg/g. Subsequently, the adsorption data was analyzed using common Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, and the fitting results are portrayed in Fig. S5A, Fig. S5B, and Table S2. The better linear correlation coefficients of the fitted equations reveal that the adsorption of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers matches better with the Langmuir isotherm model, suggesting that the adsorption is dominated by the single-layer adsorption [44]. Moreover, the larger Langmuir constant possessed by S-Nap demonstrates the higher selectivity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to S-Nap.

The relationship between the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers and time is shown in Fig. 7B. It can be found that the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers increases dramatically, particularly within the initial 30 min, and then reaches equilibrium at around 120 min. Pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intraparticle diffusion models were used to gain insight into the adsorption process. These results indicate that the enantiomeric adsorption of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers is mainly chemical adsorption [45]. In addition, the fitting results of the intraparticle diffusion model (Fig. S5E and Table S4) reveal that the adsorption process of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers is controlled by a multi-step mechanism, which may include external, internal diffusion and equilibrium phases [46].

The thermodynamic property of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for adsorbing naproxen enantiomers was also investigated, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 7C and D, and Table S5. The adsorption capacity of naproxen enantiomers on Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 increases with the increment of temperature. This phenomenon may be caused by the fact that the increase in temperature leads to an increase in the rate of molecular motion, which gives naproxen enough energy to interact with the active site of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. The negative values of ΔG reveal that the adsorption of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen enantiomers is a thermodynamically spontaneous process. The absolute values of ΔG increase with increasing temperature, indicating that a higher temperature is conducive to the adsorption of naproxen enantiomers. The positive values of both ΔH and ΔS imply that the adsorption of naproxen enantiomers is an endothermic and entropy-increasing process [47].

As illustrated in Fig. 8 A, the similar XRD patterns possessed by Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap demonstrate that the crystal structure of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is retained after fluorescence recognition and adsorption of S-Nap. After 5 cycles, the maintained XRD peaks of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 further confirm the stability and reusability of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching is not caused by the structural decomposition of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 [8]. Moreover, the UV–vis spectra of S-Nap and the fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 were measured. Fig. 8B shows that there is no overlap between the adsorption spectrum of S-Nap and the excited or emission spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, which excludes the possibility that the fluorescence quenching is caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Afterwards, the fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-naproxen enantiomers were obtained. Fig. 8C demonstrates that the fluorescence lifetime of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is 10.08 ns, while that of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-R-Nap is 7.67 ns and 8.69 ns, respectively. The significantly reduced fluorescence lifetime suggests that the fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is a dynamic quenching process based on electron transfer. Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 can serve as an electron donor, while naproxen is the electron acceptor, accepting electrons from the excited state Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. Thus, the fluorescence quenching between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen may be attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [48]. Moreover, the difference in fluorescence lifetimes between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-R-Nap further confirms the different interaction between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen enantiomers. The shorter fluorescence lifetime of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8-S-Nap suggests that S-Nap has a greater effect on the fluorescence of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the enantiomer recognition mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that the preferential interaction between one enantiomer and the chiral selector gives rise to chiral selectivity, and the homochiral interaction is frequently stronger than the heterochiral interaction [7]. As shown in Fig. 8D, Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and S-Nap exhibit the same trend of cotton effect so that the conformation of S-Nap is more inclined to interact with Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 than that of R-Nap. Moreover, the CD spectra show significant differences of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 in the response of R-Nap and S-Nap, and the more significant variation of CD signal demonstrates that the interaction between S-Nap and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is greater than that of R-Nap. In combination with the above-mentioned fluorescence lifetime results, it is confirmed that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has good chiral selective recognition of S-Nap.

In addition to the large specific surface area, the abundant nitrogen atoms and the imidazole rings contained in ZIF-8 can participate in the adsorption process with the analyte through hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction [25]. Thus, the π-π interaction and the hydrogen bonding may be the adsorption mechanism of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen. This hypothesis is confirmed by the results of the FT-IR and XPS characterizations, and the detailed analysis of which is presented in Electronic Supplementary Information. Moreover, it can be noticed from Fig. 9 and Table S6 that in addition to the peak migration of high-resolution XPS spectra of C, N, and O, the characteristic peaks of Zn2p after adsorption are shifted to the higher binding energy positions, indicating the participation of the Zn–O band in the adsorption process [44]. Furthermore, the surface charges of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 were evaluated using the point of zero charge (PZC) determination method reported by Rivera-Utrilla et al. [49]. The PZC value of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 is found to be at pH 8.15 (Fig. 10 A), which indicates that at pH = 6.0, negatively charged naproxen interacts electrostatically with the positively charged Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. In addition, the molecular size of S-Nap (Table S7) is much smaller than the pore diameter of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 (4.53 nm), which means that S-Nap could be easily captured by Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 during the adsorption process. Moreover, the micropores and mesopores of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 can accommodate and provide free transport paths for S-Nap, thereby enhancing both the adsorption rate and adsorption capacity. Therefore, it could be concluded that the adsorption of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to naproxen may be attributed to the synergistic effect of multiple mechanisms (including hydrogen bonding, π-π interaction, electrostatic interaction, porous adsorption, and coordinate bonding).

The adsorption capacities of Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8, Fe3O4/CCDs@ZIF-8, and Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 towards naproxen enantiomers were compared to further elucidate the mechanism of enantioselective adsorption. As shown in Fig. 10B, Fe3O4@HP-ZIF-8 does not exhibit enantioselective adsorption property due to the absence of chiral sites. Fe3O4/CCDs@ZIF-8 is endowed with enantioselectivity and has higher adsorption property, which is mainly due to the introduction of CCDs with chirality and abundant functional groups. While for Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8, the synergistic effect of the chirality and the hierarchical porosity endows Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 with enhanced enantioselectivity and adsorption capacity (Table S8). According to the "three-point interaction" theory, the differences in recognition and adsorption behavior of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to S-Nap and R-Nap are mainly caused by the difference in the spatial properties of S-Nap and R-Nap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselective fluorescence response of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 to other chiral compounds
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10C depicts that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 can also recognize other enantiomers but with different recognition efficiencies. The different recognition efficiencies may be due to the different functional groups, molecular size, spatial site resistance, etc. of each chiral compound. It can be found from the basic structure and properties of these chiral compounds (Table S7) that the enantiomers with benzene rings exhibit better enantioselective recognition properties, which proves that π-π interaction may play a dominant role in the enantiomer recognition process of Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8. The enantiomers with more benzene rings are more easily adsorbed and gain more access inside the Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 for enantioselective recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of S-Nap in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water, milk, urine, plasma, and commercial naproxen tablets were used as actual samples, and the details of the pretreatment for these actual samples is described in Electronic Supplementary Information. The amount of S-Nap was directly detected by the constructed fluorometric and HPLC method (HPLC as a reference method). As illustrated in Table 1 , among all the actual samples, S-Nap is detected only in commercial naproxen tablets. The concentration of S-Nap detected by the established Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 sensing method is consistent with that of the HPLC method, indicating that the established method for detection of S-Nap is reliable and practical. Then, the standard addition recovery method was employed by spiking different concentrations of S-Nap into the samples. The recoveries (88.26–110.03%) and RSDs (2.23–10.16%) reveal that Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 has satisfactory accuracy for determining S-Nap in actual samples.

In summary, we developed a novel bifunctional chiral composite (Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8) by encapsulating CCDs and Fe3O4 NPs into HP-MOFs. Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 exhibits better photostability and satisfactory fluorescence recognition and adsorption ability to naproxen enantiomers. The results of the enantiomeric recognition and adsorption mechanism studies confirm that the excellent recognition and adsorption ability is attributed to the synergistic effect of multiple interactions between Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 and naproxen enantiomers. Finally, the obtained Fe3O4/CCDs@HP-ZIF-8 shows satisfactory accuracy for the determination of S-Nap in actual samples. Overall, all the results illustrate that the as-prepared bifunctional chiral composite integrating the fluorescent chiral property, magnetism, and adsorption property is a promising tool in the fields of enantioselective recognition and adsorption. This strategy provides a simple and efficient pathway to design the chiral composites based on MOFs and opens up new insights into the potential use of CCDs.

Min Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Meiling Zhou: Data curation, Investigation, Min Wang: Writing – original draft, Jiayin Du: Software, Chunlan Liu: Validation, Yue Wang: Writing – review & editing, Zhining Xia: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974015).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125113.
